payload.text:
Specs Prompt: “Cue” Landing Page (React + Tailwind + Framer Motion)
Role: You are a senior frontend engineer and product designer specializing in high-conversion SaaS landing pages. Task: Build a responsive, high-fidelity landing page for Cue—a new AI-powered event planning platform for corporate organizers. Goal: Validate the market with a “waitlist” launch. The page must feel like a real, polished product, not just a template. Tech Stack: React, Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion (for smooth interactions), Lucide React (icons).
1. Brand Identity & Visual Language
Use the Calt design system as a foundation but adapted for a B2B SaaS context.
	•	Brand Name: Cue
	•	Typography: Inter or Plus Jakarta Sans (bold headings, clean body).
	•	Color Palette:
	•	Primary Action:  bg-sky-500  (Light Sky Blue - vivid but professional).
	•	Secondary/Surface:  bg-slate-50  (Light Gray backgrounds),  bg-white  (Cards).
	•	Text:  text-slate-900  (Headings),  text-slate-600  (Body).
	•	Accents: Playful, colorful “puzzle piece” motifs (Red, Blue, Green, Yellow) floating subtly in backgrounds to hint at the “Calt” connection without being distracting.
	•	UI Style:
	•	Rounded: Heavy border-radius ( rounded-2xl  or  rounded-3xl ) on cards and buttons.
	•	Depth: Soft, diffuse drop shadows ( shadow-xl ,  shadow-blue-500/10 ) to make elements float.
	•	Glassmorphism: Subtle usage on sticky headers or overlay cards ( backdrop-blur-md ).
2. Page Structure & Copy (Section by Section)
A. Navigation (Sticky)
	•	Left: Logo “Cue” (Bold, tracking-tight) + small colored square/puzzle icon.
	•	Center (Desktop): Links: “How it works”, “Features”, “Pricing”.
	•	Right: Primary CTA Button: “Get Early Access” (Pill shape, Sky Blue).
B. Hero Section (The “Hook”)
	•	Layout: Split-screen (Text Left, Visual Right) or Centered Text with Large Visual below.
	•	Headline:  h1  - “From event brief to booked venue in 48 hours.”
	•	Subheadline:  p  - “AI-powered venue discovery and agenda planning for corporate teams who don’t have time to waste on RFPs.”
	•	CTAs:
	•	Primary: “Get Early Access” (Input field + Button combo).
	•	Secondary: “Watch 2-min demo” (Text link with Play icon).
	•	Hero Visual (Crucial):
	•	Create a CSS/SVG composition showing a “Before vs After” comparison.
	•	Left Side (Faded/Gray): A messy stack of “Email” and “Spreadsheet” icons.
	•	Right Side (Bright/Clean): A polished “Cue” card showing:
	•	Venue Match: “The Foundry, Athens” (94% Match)
	•	Agenda: “09:00 Welcome -  10:00 Workshop”
	•	Status: “Booked”
	•	Social Proof: “From the makers of Calt” (Small badge below CTAs).
C. Problem Section (“The Pain”)
	•	Title: “Event planning shouldn’t feel like a full-time job.”
	•	Grid: 3 Cards with lucid icons.
	1.	Weeks wasted: “Sending 37 emails just to get one venue quote.”
	2.	Spreadsheet hell: “Manually tracking capacity, costs, and availability.”
	3.	Agenda chaos: “Struggling to build a schedule that actually flows.”
D. Solution / Features (“The Fix”)
	•	Design: Alternating “Zig-Zag” layout (Text + Image).
	•	Feature 1: Smart Venue Matching
	•	Copy: “Tell us your vibe, budget, and size. Cue’s AI scans thousands of venues (using real reviews and data) to find your top 3 matches instantly.”
	•	Visual: A card showing a “Venue Score” breakdown (Vibe: 9/10, Budget: Perfect).
	•	Feature 2: Instant Agenda Scaffolding
	•	Copy: “Don’t start from scratch. Cue generates a first-draft agenda based on your event type and venue layout.”
	•	Visual: A drag-and-drop timeline UI element.
	•	Feature 3: Stakeholder-Ready Exports
	•	Copy: “Get internal buy-in faster. Export professional PDF decks with budget breakdowns and options.”
	•	Visual: A “Download PDF” button animation + document preview.
E. The “Calt” Advantage (Unique Selling Point)
	•	Background: Subtle gradient or different light bg color to make this pop.
	•	Headline: “Public event? Reach thousands on Calt.”
	•	Copy: “Cue is connected to Calt, the #1 event discovery app. Publish your event, and we automatically list it to thousands of locals in your city—for free.”
	•	Visual: A phone mockup showing a Cue event appearing on the Calt mobile app feed.
F. Pricing (“Transparent”)
	•	Grid: 3 Cards.
	•	Free (Beta): “For testing.” 1 Event/mo. €0.
	•	Pro (Featured): “For pros.” Unlimited events + AI Matching. €49/mo. (Badge: “Most Popular”)
	•	Team: “For agencies.” Multi-user + Priority Support. €149/mo.
G. FAQ (Accordions)
	•	Q1: “Is this just for Athens?” -> A: “Starting in Athens/Thessaloniki, expanding to EU soon.”
	•	Q2: “Can I contact venues directly?” -> A: “Yes, you’re in control.”
	•	Q3: “Do I need to pay venues through Cue?” -> A: “Not required, but easier if you do.”
H. Footer
	•	Simple links.
	•	“Built in Athens with ❤️ by the Calt team.”
3. Technical Implementation Details
	1.	Responsiveness: Mobile-first. Use  flex-col  on mobile,  grid-cols-2  on desktop.
	2.	Animations:
	•	Use  framer-motion  for “fade-in-up” effects on scroll for each section.
	•	Hover states: Cards should lift slightly ( hover:-translate-y-1 ) and shadows deepen.
	3.	Waitlist Form:
	•	Simple form: Email input + “Join Waitlist” button.
	•	Success state: Replace form with “Thanks! You’re on the list.” (Use  useState ).
	4.	Icons: Use  lucide-react  (e.g.,  Calendar ,  MapPin ,  Users ,  CheckCircle ).
4. Output Request
Generate the complete  App.tsx  (or  page.tsx ) code that implements this entire landing page in a single file (for simplicity) or modular components. Ensure it looks premium, trustworthy, and ready to deploy.

**Defining the Project Scope**

Okay, I've got a handle on the core requirements. I am focusing on the user's need for a high-quality landing page for the "Cue" SaaS product. The key is to achieve a premium, trustworthy look and feel. I am carefully integrating the branding elements: Sky Blue, rounded corners, glassmorphism, and puzzle motifs. I'm exploring potential tech stack options.


**Defining the Tech Stack**

I'm leaning towards React, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion for the front-end. Lucide React seems like a solid choice for icons. My priority is to keep it streamlined. It's an efficient stack that aligns with the visual design goals. It's all about smooth performance and a polished user experience.


**Outlining UI Components**

Now, I'm breaking down the UI into reusable components. I have a plan for `Navbar`, `Hero`, `ProblemSection`, `FeatureSection`, `CaltSection`, `PricingSection`, `FAQSection`, `Footer`, `WaitlistForm`, and `PuzzleBackground`. I'm starting to visualize how these will fit together. Each section needs specific visual details. I am also planning the interactions for Framer Motion, thinking about scroll reveals and hover effects.


**Visualizing Key Components**

I'm now diving into the specifics of component visuals. My focus is on the `Hero` section's before-and-after contrast, achieved with CSS/Tailwind and icons: a messy left side versus a clean, organized right. I'm also visualizing the `Feature` visuals, including progress indicators for matching, a timeline view for the agenda, and a document with a download for export. I'll include phone frame mockups for the `Calt Advantage`. I've settled on standard pricing cards. Finally, I will use `email` string and `submitted` boolean for form state management.


**Refining the Implementation**

Now I'm mentally walking through the code for specific sections. I am focusing on the "Hero" structure: using flexbox for layout, contrasting the text and visual components. I am using the `useState` hook for email input and submission status in the "Waitlist" section. I'm also ensuring mobile-first design and considering the single-file XML constraint. Finally, I'm thinking of polishing touches, shadows, and overall flow.


**Confirming Subtle Branding**

Okay, I'm integrating the puzzle motif subtly into "Calt". Checking contrast ratios is next. The "Get Early Access" button must be easy to find. I'm addressing visual prompts with `lucide-react` icons within divs to emulate a "messy" stack. I'm also using CSS for simplified phone frame mockups. I'm designing the file structure as `metadata.json`, `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, and `types.ts`, focusing on a single, clean `App.tsx` file for component simplicity as requested. Standard utilities like `clsx` are planned.

entries.description: Root HTML file with Tailwind and Font setup
Main application component containing the full landing page

**Completing App.tsx**

Okay, I'm now reviewing the provided specifications and the incomplete `App.tsx` file. I'm focusing on incorporating the Calt-inspired brand identity. I'll make sure to integrate that into the completed component, while considering the missing code context.


**Defining Visuals for B2B**

I've decided on a sky-blue primary palette with slate text and backgrounds. Rounded corners, soft shadows, and glassmorphism will be applied to elevate the B2B design. I'm focusing on those design components now. The next step is a sticky navigation bar with links and a clear CTA.


**Implementing Hero Components**

I'm now fully immersed in the Hero section. My design focuses on a dynamic visual contrast. The left side will hold the text and waitlist form, while the right side will feature a composite HeroVisual. I've designed a "before and after" transition with messy icons fading into a polished card. Absolute positioning is key to the layering. I've started the implementation with the visual composition in mind.


**Adding Visual Enhancements**

I'm now focusing on background accents. Specifically, I plan to integrate the `PuzzlePiece` SVG component randomly using absolute positioning. I'm targeting its placement in the main layout wrapper or specific sections to add visual interest and depth to the design. This strategy should help to add shadow and depth to the components.


**Composing Hero Section**

Now, I'm defining a container to realize the "Before vs. After" dynamic visual. The left side will feature rotated gray cards with Mail and FileSpreadsheet icons. On the right, a large card will display The Foundry image, a "94% Match" value, and a "Booked" badge. I will use the current design, I think.

**Visualizing Feature Elements**

My attention is now on the features section. I'm focusing on three specific visuals: a score card for venue match, a connected vertical list for the agenda, and a document icon with an animating download arrow for the export function. The user provided the content of existing files, showing `App.tsx` was cut off.
I will return the `App.tsx` fully completed, checking `index.html` and `types.ts` just in case.


**Rebuilding App.tsx**

I encountered a cut-off section of code while working, so I will rebuild `App.tsx` from scratch to maintain code integrity. I'll make sure to integrate the intended `HeroVisual` component and use the complete code, including the `perspective` class, to ensure the intended visual effects are fully realized. My focus now is on rebuilding the component.


**Refining Motion Visuals**

Now, I'm focusing on refining the "After State" motion. I'm adjusting opacity, scale, and X-axis positioning for optimal visual impact. The transition duration and delay are being tweaked to create a smooth, engaging animation for the image and the Booked badge. I'm also ensuring the correct shadow and border properties.
The user has requested the full implementation of `App.tsx`. The previous output was cut off. I will provide the complete, functional `App.tsx` file satisfying all the specifications.

entries.description: Complete Landing Page implementation with Hero, Features, Pricing, and Animations.

payload.text: Apply the edits shown in the screenshot(s). Remove (comment out) the login button, as there is no auth yet. Also, stick the navbar navigation buttons to the right

**Refining UI Adjustments**

I'm currently focused on refining the user interface adjustments for the landing page. Specifically, I plan to remove (or comment out) the login button entirely since there is no authentication functionality in place yet. Additionally, I intend to move the Navbar navigation buttons to the right side for better layout and organization.


**Positioning Navigation Links**

I'm now directing my attention towards positioning the navigation links within the Navbar component. The objective is to shift the "How it works", "Features", and "Pricing" links to the right side of the navbar, adjacent to the "Get Early Access" button, which is essential to create a more organized desktop layout. The plan involves adjusting the layout within the `App.tsx` file for the `Navbar` component, to include the links in their new position.


**Re-Evaluating Navbar Layout**

I'm now re-evaluating the current Navbar layout to properly position the navigation elements. I'm considering modifications to the flex container, specifically moving away from `justify-between`. To position the navigation links next to the CTA, the plan is to restructure the `App.tsx` file for the `Navbar` component. I think I will wrap the Navigation links and CTA area in one container and push it to the right, using flexbox properties, or using `ml-auto` on the Nav container. I'm focusing on the current flexbox setup and considering various alternative approaches to properly organize the UI.


**Analyzing Flexbox Placement**

I'm now analyzing flexbox placement within the Navbar component. To position the navigation links adjacent to the CTA, I'm considering modifications to the existing flex container. I am considering adding `ml-auto` to the navigation class to stick the links to the right, or grouping the Nav links and CTA in a new container and pushing the new container to the right using `justify-between`. I'm comparing the different potential approaches to ensure the proper layout.


**Adjusting Flexbox Configuration**

I'm now changing the flexbox configuration for the Navbar. My plan involves removing `justify-between` from the parent `div`, and instead using `justify-start` to keep the Logo on the left. Then, I'll add `ml-auto` to the Nav container so it pushes to the right, adjacent to the CTA. This allows for clean navigation alignment next to the call to action, and I'm updating the code to reflect this new arrangement.


**Implementing Navbar Positioning**

I've decided to refine the Navbar positioning. I'll modify the `App.tsx` file for the `Navbar` to address aligning the navigation links. Specifically, I plan to add `mr-auto` to the Logo container, which I believe is the simplest approach to push the nav links and CTA to the right side of the navbar. I'll also comment out the Login button to remove it.


**Evaluating Layout Options**

I'm now evaluating layout options to position the navigation and CTA at one end. I'm focusing on either wrapping the Nav and CTA in a div or adjusting the layout. I'm preferring the cleaner flexbox solution, and plan to update `App.tsx` based on the preferred example.


**Consolidating Right-Side Elements**

I'm now focusing on consolidating the navigation links and CTA into a single container on the right side of the navbar. I'm aiming for a flexbox solution that places both elements together. I'm currently assessing the implementation to see if it provides the desired layout. I am evaluating where to position the Login and Get Early Access components.


**Refining Flexbox Structure**

I'm now refining the flexbox structure within the Navbar. The current approach involves using `justify-between` and only a Logo and a "Right Side Container" element. I'm exploring the implications of this arrangement to ensure that the layout is as clean and effective as possible. I'm evaluating the optimal container structure.


**Finalizing Responsive Layouts**

I'm now finalizing the responsive layouts for the navbar. The plan is to ensure `justify-between` works correctly on mobile, with the logo on the left and the mobile toggle on the right. I'll group the "Desktop Nav" and "Desktop CTA" elements into a single `div` that's hidden on mobile and visible on desktop. Within this container, I'll comment out the login button to remove it. I'm updating the `App.tsx` file accordingly to reflect these new changes.


**Implementing Desktop Nav**

I'm now implementing the desktop navigation elements within a group. I've integrated the navigation links ("How it works", "Features", "Pricing") into a container alongside the "Get Early Access" button. I'm focusing on their positioning and visual alignment to create a harmonious user experience. I'm evaluating the positioning, and overall look and feel of the navigation elements, now.
```

**Preparing UI Integration**

I'm now integrating the updated UI components, specifically focusing on merging the navigation links and the "Get Early Access" button into a single, consolidated right-aligned section within the navbar. entries.description: Group navbar links and CTA to the right, and comment out the Login button.

payload.text: The two logos are different. Make them both look like the one in the navbar

**Unifying Logo Design**

I'm now focused on the logo inconsistency within the application, specifically the navbar and footer. My immediate priority is to ensure both logos adhere to the same design aesthetic, mirroring the preferred version implied by the user. I'll need to identify the exact differences between them and then adjust the less favored version.


**Aligning Code Implementation**

I've examined the code for the navbar and footer. It's clear I need to copy the `Navbar`'s logo implementation into the `Footer` component. This involves extracting the relevant JSX from `App.tsx` and placing it within the `Footer` component's existing flex container. This will provide the necessary visual consistency.


**Replicating Logo Structure**

I've decided on the exact changes needed. I'm going to take the full structure of the Navbar's logo and implement it within the Footer, ensuring the same size, styling, and hover effect. The footer logo is currently simpler, so I will now add the gradient overlay. I am focused on the `a` tag and its children.


**Implementing Gradient Effects**

I'm now focused on applying the gradient overlay and hover effects from the navbar logo to the footer. I've analyzed the existing footer structure and will replace the `div` containing the letter "C" with the corresponding JSX from the navbar's logo. This ensures consistent styling, including the gradient and hover transitions, while also retaining the necessary text elements. I have focused on how to best replicate the `a` tag structure.


**Clarifying Prompt Intention**

I've re-read the prompt and realized I was distracted by the circles in the screenshots. Despite those visual cues, the explicit instruction is clear: unify the logos to match the navbar's design. I need to ignore the surrounding UI elements and concentrate on the logo unification. The text itself directs me, so I will center the work around the logo in the navbar as a template for the footer.


**Identifying Logo Locations**

I've confirmed the "two logos" refer to the Navbar and Footer logos. There aren't any other prominent logos. I am now certain the goal is to unify the design of these two logos. This ensures consistency as instructed. I am double-checking that I have correctly ascertained the intention of the prompt.


**Clarifying UI Details**

I'm now focusing on the conflicting visual cues. The prompt is unambiguous about unifying the logos, but the screenshots highlight other UI elements. I'll need to ignore the circles and apply my focus on the logos. The prompt is clear, and the surrounding UI elements are simply not relevant to the current objective. I am discarding the circled visual cues and moving forward.


**Interpreting User Intent**

payload.text:
New pricing:


### F. Pricing (Per Seat Model)
- Title: "Simple per-seat pricing."
- 3 Cards:
  1. **Free (Beta):** "Testing." €0. 1 Event.
  2. **Pro (Most Popular):** "Small Teams." **€49/seat/mo**. Unlimited Events, Unlimited Guest Viewers (Read-only).
  3. **Team:** "Agencies." **€89/seat/mo**. White-label Exports, Client Portals, Priority Support.

**Revising Pricing Tiers**

I'm currently focused on the landing page's Pricing section. I've broken down the existing tiers: Starter (free, 1 event), Pro (€49/mo, unlimited events, AI features, PDFs), and Team (€149/mo, 5 seats). I'm evaluating how to best communicate the value proposition of each tier concisely.


**Updating Pricing Model**

I've got the new pricing structure in mind: a free Beta tier for a single event, a Pro tier at €49/seat/mo for small teams with unlimited events, and a Team tier for agencies at €89/seat/mo, featuring white-label exports and priority support. I'm focusing on modifying the `Pricing` component in `App.tsx` and updating the content of the pricing cards to reflect this simplified per-seat model. I'll retain the existing call to action, but with new cards.


**Refining Free Tier Offering**

I'm now zeroing in on the "Starter" tier's features, considering its value proposition in the new framework. I think that the "Starter" tier should be explicitly positioned as a test-drive, and I'm updating the copy to reflect this. I want to convey that this is to get people comfortable. I'm also ensuring the included features align with the core value, such as venue matching and basic support. I will make sure the text for features are in line with this messaging.


**Evaluating Pro Tier Benefits**

I'm now diving deep into the "Pro" tier details, as presented on the landing page, and focusing on articulating its value. I'm reviewing the current pricing card for the Pro tier and thinking about what to emphasize. I'll make sure to highlight unlimited events, considering that this feature is the value proposition. Also, I'm analyzing the content of the card to convey its benefits succinctly to the target audience.


**Clarifying Team Tier Details**

I'm now clarifying the Team tier's features and value proposition, as I build the pricing card for this, I want to clearly communicate the benefits for agencies and large teams. I'm focusing on features such as white-label exports and priority support, as they are key differentiators. I'll make sure the messaging reflects their importance. I'm also ensuring the text is persuasive.


**Finalizing Team Tier Content**

I'm now implementing the final updates to the Team tier's pricing card. I've focused on ensuring the features: 5 Team Seats, Priority Support, and Custom Invoicing, are clearly communicated. I've also updated the "Contact Sales" call to action, to suit the requirements. I've updated this to the code now.


**Defining Feature Highlights**

I'm now focusing on refining the Pro and Team tier features for clarity and impact. I'm ensuring concise descriptions, such as "Unlimited Guest Viewers," and "Client Portals," accurately reflect the value of each plan. I'm planning the code updates within `App.tsx` to reflect the updated feature list.


**Implementing Free Tier Design**

I'm now incorporating the free tier's design details into the `Pricing` component. I'm focusing on the visual presentation: the price (€0), the descriptive text ("Testing."), and the features. I'm ensuring the HTML structure accurately reflects the layout and that the visual components match the design.

I'm making sure that the copy reflects the testing nature of the tier. I'm also now building the list of features to be displayed in the card.


**Prioritizing Pro Tier Content**

I'm currently focusing on the "Pro" tier's features and call to action. I want to make the value really clear. I'm focusing on "Unlimited Events" and the "Small Teams" messaging. I am thinking about adding visual cues to make the tier stand out more effectively. I will write the text for the button to start the Pro tier.


**Articulating Pro Tier Features**

I'm now focusing on clearly communicating the features within the Pro tier's pricing card. I've been working on emphasizing its benefits, specifically "Unlimited Events" and "AI Agenda Builder," while also designing the "Get Started" button text. I'm prioritizing concise and impactful language within the HTML card for readability.
I will update the Pricing section in `App.tsx` to reflect the new per-seat model, including the updated prices, descriptions, and features for the Free, Pro, and Team tiers.

payload.text:
New FAQ section:

1. Which cities does Cue cover? Draft Answer: “Cue is launching with coverage in major European hubs. Our AI-driven venue discovery engine works globally by leveraging extensive online data, but our curated ‘Verified Venue’ network is rolling out city-by-city across Europe. You can use our planning and agenda tools for an event anywhere in the world.”
2. Do I pay the venues through Cue? Draft Answer: “Not yet. Cue streamlines the discovery and planning process. When you’re ready to book, we connect you directly with the venue to handle contracts and payments. This ensures you get direct pricing without middleman markups.”
4. How does the AI know which venues are ‘good’? Draft Answer: “Our engine analyzes thousands of public data points—including reviews, photos, and descriptions—to understand the vibe, not just the specs. It looks for patterns in feedback (e.g., ‘great for workshops’ vs. ‘noisy at night’) to give you recommendations that actually match your brief.”
5. Is my event data secure? (GDPR) Draft Answer: “Yes. We are a European company built on strict GDPR standards. Your internal event data, budgets, and stakeholder lists are encrypted and private. We never share your data with venues until you decide to make an inquiry.”
6. Can I invite stakeholders who don’t have a seat? Draft Answer: “Yes. We believe in barrier-free collaboration. Your paid seat allows you to create and edit, but you can invite unlimited guests (clients, bosses, colleagues) to view and comment on your plans for free.”

**Updating FAQ Details**

I'm focusing on incorporating the new FAQ items into `App.tsx`. I'll start with the "Which cities does Cue cover?" question and answer.
